headline: The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft: Where Champions Are Born and Dreams Go to Die
subheadline: Bobby Witt Jr. goes first overall, the defending champs make a statement, and at least three managers are already planning their 2027 strategy.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a moment in every fantasy draft — usually around pick 47, when someone takes a closer two rounds early and the chat explodes with laugh-react emojis — where you realize this isn't just about baseball anymore. It's about ego, vindication, and the primal urge to prove you're smarter than 13 other people who've been staring at the same spreadsheets since January. The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft was that moment stretched across 210 picks and approximately 47 questionable decisions.

If this draft were a prestige TV series, it would be *Succession* — old money trying to protect their dynasties (**Sugar Land Skeeters**, defending champs), new blood attempting hostile takeovers (**North Correa**, finally picking first after back-to-back basement finishes), and at least one team making decisions that feel like Kendall Roy trying to rap at a wedding. The big storyline? **Bobby Witt Jr.** going 1.1 over the consensus top player in fantasy baseball, **Shohei Ohtani**. The steals were real (someone got **Gunnar Henderson** at pick 17 — SEVENTEEN!). The reaches were painful (we'll get there). And somewhere in the middle rounds, championships were either won or lost while managers were probably checking their group chat instead of their draft queue.

Let's break down every single pick, roast the decisions that deserve roasting, and crown our preseason favorite — because that's never backfired spectacularly, right?

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The snake draft gods smiled upon **North Correa** with the first overall pick, which feels cosmically appropriate after consecutive finishes of 14th and 12th. (That's the fantasy equivalent of being gifted a Porsche after totaling two Camrys.) They went with **Bobby Witt Jr.** — a defensible choice given his 2025 slash line of .332/.389/.556 with 32 homers, 94 RBI, and 31 stolen bases. The guy is basically a cheat code in categories leagues. Still, passing on **Ohtani** (54 HR, 135 RBI, a 1.007 OPS in 2025) raised eyebrows. The **Athletic Park Dragons** happily scooped up Shohei at 2, and the rest of the first round played out relatively chalk: **Mookie Betts** (3), **Aaron Judge** (4), **Elly De La Cruz** (5), **José Ramírez** (6), **Juan Soto** (7), **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** (8).

The real chaos started in the back half of Round 1. **General Hospital** took **Francisco Lindor** at 10 — reasonable! Then **Sugar Land Skeeters** grabbed **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 11, because when you're the defending champs, you draft vibes as much as value. But the real head-scratcher? **Benchwarmers** sitting at 14 and taking **Julio Rodríguez**, whose 2025 campaign (.258/.315/.421, 22 HR) looked more like "talented guy figuring it out" than "top-15 pick." Meanwhile, **Gunnar Henderson** — the guy who slashed .301/.377/.548 with 35 homers and 24 steals — fell to pick 17. In a 14-team league. I'm convinced someone's draft software crashed.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa (Draft Pick #1)**

The perennial basement dwellers finally got their golden ticket, and they didn't waste it. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1.1 gives them the best all-around fantasy asset who isn't splitting time between pitcher and hitter. **Pete Alonso** (34 HR, 88 RBI in 2025) at 2.28 provides the RBI safety net, while **Logan Gilbert** (3.42 ERA, 1.08 WHIP, 190 K) anchors the rotation. But here's where it gets spicy: **Spencer Strider** at 9.113 is either a masterstroke or a disaster — the guy missed significant time in 2024 and threw just 98 innings in 2025 (though with a 2.84 ERA). **Luis Robert Jr.** at 11.141 is a classic "buying the talent discount" move; he hit .218 in 2025 but still flashed 25-homer power. The upside is immense. The floor is the floor of the 2024 White Sox clubhouse. **Grade: B+** (The ceiling here is a title; the floor is another long autumn.)

**Athletic Park Dragons (Draft Pick #2)**

Getting **Ohtani** at 2 is like finding out your ex's rebound is way less attractive — validation feels good. They paired him with **Matt Olson** (28 HR, but a disappointing .231 AVG in 2025) and **Kyle Schwarber** (.197 AVG, but 38 bombs), which means they're punting batting average harder than an NFL team in prevent defense. The pitching strategy is fascinating: **Michael King** (2.95 ERA), **Shota Imanaga** (3.21 ERA as a rookie), and **Aaron Nola** (3.57 ERA) give them three legitimate starters by Round 6. **Luis Castillo** at 7.86 is a sneaky value play — 184 strikeouts in 2025 despite the 3.89 ERA. But they waited until pick 114 for a catcher (**Will Smith**), which feels late. After finishing 6th in 2024 and collapsing to 14th in 2025, this feels like a boom-or-bust construction. **Grade: B** (If the bats hit, they contend; if Olson and Schwarber both slump, they're toast.)

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers (Draft Pick #3)**

A rookie manager in their second year, coming off a 10th-place finish, and they make... the safest possible picks? **Mookie Betts** (4.8 WAR, .891 OPS in 2025) at 3 is unimpeachable. **Ketel Marte** (.298/.366/.513) at 26 provides 2B eligibility gold. **William Contreras** at 31 is early for a catcher, but the guy hit .308 with 25 homers — he's the best hitting catcher in baseball. The concern? They went HEAVY on relievers in the back half: **Justin Martinez**, **Jeff Hoffman**, **Jason Foley**, **Jason Adam**. Four relievers in the last six rounds is a strategy, I guess. It's not one I endorse, but it's a strategy. **Grade: B-** (Solid floor, questionable ceiling; they'll finish somewhere between 7th and 10th again.)

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Draft Pick #4)**

THE DEFENDING... wait, no. The 2024 CHAMPS who fell to 6th last year. **Aaron Judge** (54 HR, .322 AVG, 6.8 WAR in 2025) at 4 is about as safe as picks get. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** at 25 is interesting — he struggled after the Yankees trade (combine for .249/.308/.453 overall), but the positional flexibility is elite. **Austin Riley** at 32 feels like a reach; he hit .272 with 30 HR, but his OBP (.331) was underwhelming. **Pablo López** anchors the rotation (3.32 ERA, 196 K), and they found value with **Spencer Schwellenbach** (2.99 ERA as a rookie) at 6.81. **Xavier Edwards** at 9.116 is a stolen base lottery ticket (42 SB in 2025). This team knows what it's doing — one championship already, consistently competitive. **Grade: A-** (Built to contend again; Judge keeps them in every week.)

**BaseballPro77 (Draft Pick #5)**

The league's other champion (2023) and perpetual contender remains annoyingly competent. **Elly De La Cruz** (31 HR, 67 SB in 2025) at 5 is the pick that wins you stolen bases by Memorial Day. **Jackson Merrill** (.292/.327/.500, 24 HR) at 24 was a slight reach based on ADP, but the kid is 21 with shortstop eligibility AND center field defense — the future is bright. **Cole Ragans** (2.89 ERA, 223 K) at 33 might be the steal of the first three rounds. Taking **Emmanuel Clase** at 52 is classic BaseballPro77 — securing the elite closer early to dominate NSVH. **Bo Bichette** at 11.145 is a buy-low masterpiece after his brutal 2025 (.241/.295/.377), and **Royce Lewis** at 12.164 is a hammock with 30-HR upside when healthy (which, admittedly, is never). **Grade: A** (This manager simply doesn't miss in the middle rounds.)

**Albuquerque Bombers (Draft Pick #6)**

Consistently competitive (5th, 10th, 3rd the last three years), the Bombers went with **José Ramírez** at 6 — still elite at 33, with a .285/.357/.536 line and 32/22 HR/SB in 2025. **Freddie Freeman** at 23 is the "I want my batting average to be fine" pick (.302, 22 HR). **Garrett Crochet** at 34 is where things get interesting — the converted reliever threw 146 innings with a 3.58 ERA and 210 strikeouts, but workload concerns loom. **Willy Adames** at 6.79 (27 HR, 94 RBI on his new Giants deal) provides plus power from shortstop. The deep sleeper? **Jasson Domínguez** at 11.146 — the Yankees' "Martian" finally stayed healthy and slashed .267/.342/.478 in limited action. If he breaks out, this team wins the league. **Grade: B+** (Veteran core with upside plays; classic contender build.)

**Hiroshima Radiation (Draft Pick #7)**

After yo-yoing between 2nd (2023), 11th (2024), and 5th (2025), the Radiation went star-heavy early. **Juan Soto** (.304/.418/.569, 41 HR) at 7 is arguably top-3 value. **Trea Turner** (.285/.342/.467, 21/28 HR/SB) at 22 pairs nicely. But **Blake Snell** at 35? The guy threw 104 innings in 2025 with a 3.12 ERA but can't stay on the field — that's a Round 5 pick at best. **Jose Altuve** at 50 feels nostalgic (he's 36 and hit .275 with 17 HR), and **Tyler Glasnow** at 63 is another glass cannon (pun absolutely intended — 2.93 ERA in 98 IP before getting hurt... again). This is the roster of a manager who drafts names over numbers. **Grade: B-** (The talent is undeniable, but the injury risk is giving 2019 fantasy nightmare fuel.)

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Draft Pick #8)**

The runners-up in 2025, consistently finishing 4th, 5th, or 2nd the last three years. These are the San Antonio Spurs of BeerLeagueBaseball — always there, never quite closing. **Vladdy Jr.** (.302/.381/.544, 37 HR) at 8 is an elite floor. **Bryce Harper** (.276/.374/.507, 30 HR) at 21 provides the "my team is never embarrassing" baseline. **Corbin Burnes** (2.92 ERA, 214 K) at 36 anchors the rotation. The real win? **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at 5.64. Yes, he played just 76 games in 2025, but when healthy he's a first-round talent. At pick 64, you're paying for Round 6 risk with Round 1 upside. **George Kirby** at 10.133 (3.19 ERA, 176 K) is pure draft day robbery. **Grade: A-** (Built to finally break through; this might be their year.)

**Buttville Skidmarks (Draft Pick #9)**

(First of all, incredible team name. Second of all, this draft is chaos.) **Kyle Tucker** (.274/.345/.551, 29 HR, 20 SB) at 9 is rock solid. **Zack Wheeler** at 20 gives them an ace (2.57 ERA, but only 130 IP due to injury). **Rafael Devers** (35 HR, .277 AVG) at 37 and **Corey Seager** (31 HR, .276) at 48 means this lineup THUMPS. But **Jacob deGrom** at 65? The dude threw 57 innings in 2025 and is being held together by prayers and Tommy John scar tissue. **Raisel Iglesias** at 76 is curious — he had 35 saves and a 2.86 ERA, but his FIP (3.89) suggests regression. **Nolan Arenado** at 15.205 is a nice flier (.251, 17 HR in a disappointing 2025), but at 35 he might be cooked. **Grade: B** (High-upside bats, but deGrom pick could haunt them.)

**General Hospital (Draft Pick #10)**

Back-to-back 13th-place finishes. The patient is critical. **Francisco Lindor** (.262/.341/.475, 31 HR) at 10 is fine. **Jarren Duran** (.294/.353/.486, 22 HR, 38 SB) at 19 is a steal — dude should've gone top 15. **Chris Sale** at 38, coming off his Cy Young campaign (2.38 ERA, 225 K), could anchor this rotation if his arm survives. But then... **Mike Trout** at 6.75. I know, I know — "you can't pass on Trout." Except he played 29 games in 2025 and hasn't been healthy since the Pleistocene era. The analytics say "avoid," the heart says "but it's MIKE TROUT," and General Hospital chose vibes. **Bryan Woo** (2.89 ERA in 102 IP) at 103 is legit value though. **Grade: C+** (The Trout pick alone costs them half a letter grade.)

**Sugar Land Skeeters (Draft Pick #11)**

THE DEFENDING CHAMPIONS. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** (.282/.351/.552, 28 HR, 22 SB) at 11 is aggressive, but champs earned the right to swing big. **Tarik Skubal** at 18 is absolutely absurd value — the guy won the AL Cy Young with a 2.07 ERA and 228 strikeouts in 192 innings. Getting him at 18 is like finding a Van Gogh at a garage sale. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** at 46 (2.66 ERA in year one) gives them a 1-2 punch that's unfair. **Roki Sasaki** at 96 — if the Dodgers' new toy figures out MLB hitters, this team might have the best rotation in the league. **Alec Bohm** at 130 (.298, 23 HR) is sneaky consistent. **Grade: A** (Champions draft like champions; Skubal at 18 might win them another ring.)

**Riverside Roughnecks (Draft Pick #12)**

Three straight finishes of 9th, 9th, and 11th. The definition of treadmill purgatory. But this draft might change things. **Jackson Chourio** (.275/.323/.484, 21 HR at age 21) at 12 is betting on the breakout. And then... **GUNNAR HENDERSON** AT PICK 17. The guy hit .301 with 35 homers and 24 steals! His ADP should've been top 10! This is the steal of Round 2, full stop. **Ozzie Albies** (.256/.305/.454, 19 HR) at 40 provides 2B stability. **Roki Sasaki** going at 96... wait, he went to Sugar Land. But they DID get **Bryce Miller** (3.44 ERA, 162 K) at 101. The Gunnar pick alone bumps this grade. **Grade: B+** (Henderson falling to 17 is a league-altering event.)

**Canberra Wombats (Draft Pick #13)**

Third place in 2023, then 12th, then 4th — the volatility is concerning. **Corbin Carroll** (.267/.342/.463 in a bounce-back 2025) at 13 has superstar potential if he rediscovers 2023. **Yordan Alvarez** (.297/.392/.583, 33 HR) at 16 is elite — arguably the best pure hitter in baseball. **Wyatt Langford** (.259/.317/.441 as a rookie) at 41 is a gamble on year-two growth. **Josh Hader** at 69 secures the closer, and **Cal Raleigh** (28 HR from the catcher position) at 72 was a steal. **Shane McClanahan** at 181 is the type of late-round lottery ticket you love — if his shoulder cooperates, he's a top-20 starter. **Grade: B+** (Yordan at 16 is quietly one of the best values in Round 2.)

**Benchwarmers (Draft Pick #14)**

Last pick in Round 1, first pick in Round 2 — the "turns" matter most. **Julio Rodríguez** at 14... I've already expressed my concerns (.258, 22 HR, below his established talent level). **Paul Skenes** at 15 is the counter-punch — the rookie phenom with a 2.18 ERA and 170 K in 133 innings is LEGIT. **Oneil Cruz** at 42 (18 HR, 33 SB, but a .232 AVG) is peak "tools over production" drafting. **Dylan Cease** at 43 (3.49 ERA, 224 K) gives them a strikeout stud. The issue? They took **Edwin Díaz** at 70 after his disastrous 2025 (5.02 ERA, 1.58 WHIP) — that's a reach for a broken closer. **Pete Crow-Armstrong** at 155 (.218 AVG but elite defense) might never hit enough. **Grade: C+** (Skenes and Cease are great; the rest is a Rorschach test.)

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE HEROES (Best Value Picks)**

1. **Tarik Skubal, Round 2, Pick 18 — Sugar Land Skeeters**: The reigning Cy Young winner fell to PICK 18. He had a 2.07 ERA. Two-point-zero-seven. In a 12-category league where QS, ERA, WHIP, and K all matter? This is larceny.

2. **Gunnar Henderson, Round 2, Pick 17 — Riverside Roughnecks**: I cannot stress this enough: a 23-year-old shortstop with a .301/.377/.548 slash line, 35 homers, 24 steals, and Gold Glove defense fell to the 17th pick. Somebody was asleep. Multiple somebodies.

3. **Ronald Acuña Jr., Round 5, Pick 64 — Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**: Yes, he's coming off an ACL tear. Yes, he only played 76 games. But he's RONALD ACUÑA JR., a guy who put up 41 HR and 73 SB just two years ago. In Round 5, you're betting on talent, and this is the most talented player available by a country mile.

**THE VILLAINS (Worst Reaches)**

1. **Mike Trout, Round 6, Pick 75 — General Hospital**: He's played 155 games total over the last three seasons. At some point, "Hall of Fame talent" has to bow to "literally cannot stay on the field." Round 6 is too rich for a player your roster might never see.

2. **Blake Snell, Round 3, Pick 35 — Hiroshima Radiation**: Snell is undeniably talented when pitching, but he hasn't thrown 150 innings since 2019. In Round 3, you need reliability. This pick screams "I remember 2023 Blake Snell" without acknowledging "2024-25 Blake Snell who can't stay healthy."

3. **Edwin Díaz, Round 5, Pick 70 — Benchwarmers**: A 5.02 ERA. A 1.58 WHIP. For a closer. In Round 5. When your league counts ERA, WHIP, AND NSVH, you cannot afford a reliever actively destroying two of those categories. This is paying for the name, not the performance.

**V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **The Sugar Land Skeeters repeat as champions**, becoming the first back-to-back winner in league history. The Skubal/Yamamoto/Tatis core is just too good, and their late-round pitching depth (**Sasaki**, **Pepiot**) provides insurance against injuries.

2. **Gunnar Henderson finishes as the #1 overall player in fantasy**, and **Riverside Roughnecks** rides that pick-17 steal to their first top-3 finish since... ever, actually.

3. **General Hospital finishes last for the third consecutive year**, because Mike Trout will play fewer than 50 games and Chris Sale's arm will file for divorce by June.

4. **Spencer Strider throws 180+ innings** and rewards **North Correa's** patience, emerging as a top-10 fantasy pitcher and finally pushing them out of the cellar.

5. **Bo Bichette's revenge tour makes BaseballPro77 look brilliant** — he returns to 2023 form (.306, 20 HR, 100+ R) and that pick-145 selection becomes the league's biggest "well, that worked out" moment.

**VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Give me the **Sugar Land Skeeters** to run it back.

Look, I know defending champions usually regress. I know there's historical precedent for "championship hangover." But this team didn't just win last year — they improved through this draft. **Tarik Skubal** at 18 is generational value. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** gives them a legitimate 1-2 punch. **Tatis** and **Cody Bellinger** provide the offensive upside, while **Brenton Doyle** (24 HR, 35 SB) quietly does everything. They've got **Alec Bohm** for counting stats, **Roki Sasaki** for strikeout upside, and **Salvador Perez** for positional scarcity.

The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** will push them — they've finished 2nd, 5th, and 4th the last three years, and this roster might be their best. **BaseballPro77** never goes away, and those mid-round selections (**Ragans**, **Bichette**, **Lewis**) give them sneaky depth. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins**, the 2024 champs, could absolutely bounce back with Judge leading the way.

But championships aren't won in March. They're won when your third starter goes down and your fourth starter steps up. They're won when your late-round lottery ticket hits. They're won when your Round 2 steal becomes a league-winning player.

Sugar Land has **Skubal** to anchor everything, the pitching depth to survive attrition, and the hitters to compete in every offensive category. They drafted with the confidence of champions and the intelligence of perpetual contenders.

So raise your MillerLite® (responsibly, of course), toast to another season of dreams and despair, and pencil in the **Sugar Land Skeeters** for banner number two.

After all, isn't that what defending champions do?

*Now, if you'll excuse me, I need to go text my league chat about how someone let Gunnar Henderson fall to pick 17. Because that's absolutely inexcusable. Truly. We'll be talking about that one in March of 2036.*
